project_info.title: 0.01 Degree
project_info.core_selling_points: One line of code saves humanity; 0.01 degree reversal twist; morally complex eco-terrorists
project_info.antagonist_tiers: 小:Joe/Dr.Freeman, 中:Mysterious Man, 大:Group Leader(TBD)
project_info.factions: Mars Government, SpaceNano Corp, National Energy Lab, Radical Animal Rights Group, Black Market
project_info.gf_irreversible_cost: If caught, Peter dies; one chance only
project_info.target_reader: Sci-fi readers interested in time travel, moral philosophy, tech thrillers

protagonist_state.name: Peter Kamau

power.bottleneck: Peter has formed private decision to act. Three integrator reference-frame priority adjustment points identified. No code written yet. Sol 75 (D-5) Vasquez walkthrough scheduled. Sol 80 (D-10) launch deadline announced. Five days to write a plan.

location.current: Bunk, residential level, Kuiper Belt Asteroid Base (Sol 70 ~2230, post observation port)

protagonist_state.emotional_state: forming-certainty under deadline; performance-under-knowledge for crew unchanged

power_system: Kerr black hole / CTC / Casimir cradle (cover-story canon)
Orbital interceptors (Ch10 reveal: non-existent / cover-story fiction)
0.01-degree adjustment (Ch10: asteroid-deflection mechanism, the entire point of the code)

active_threads: Particle accelerator acquisition via Dr. Freeman
Three technologies converging on Kuiper Belt shuttle

foreshadowing.note: Orbital interceptors named explicitly by Kessler as if they exist; Ch10 reveal that no such system exists.
foreshadowing.carry_note_ch9: Reader-held; Peter has not yet tested. Carries to Ch10.
foreshadowing.payoff_note: Confirmed non-existent. Frame inversion of catch-side-pass-1 trajectory geometry exposes the same vectors as the Chicxulub impactor pre-impact deflection envelope; the orbital interceptors named by Kessler in Ch8 do not and never did exist.

foreshadowing.note: Kessler deep-time / Jupiter-chaos academic-passion bleed-through (line 79 + softened line 81). Reader-perceptible, not Peter-detected.
foreshadowing.payoff_note: Kessler's academic identity made explicit: 'I was a marine biologist before I was anything else' / 'a paleontologist of a sort'.

foreshadowing.note: Kessler mirror-cue: 'I've been wanting to talk to someone who sees the system the way you see it' (paraphrased reader-cue).
foreshadowing.payoff_note: Kessler tells Peter why he is telling him: 'Thank you for being someone I could tell.' / 'someone who could be told it without being lost'.

foreshadowing.note: Third thing deferred by Kessler at meeting close (after launch trajectory phase + interceptor approach geometry).
foreshadowing.carry_note_ch9: Kessler again defers in Ch9 office reveal: 'We'll talk about the third thing soon.'
foreshadowing.carry_note_ch10: Sol 67 afternoon: Peter passes Kessler closed office door, strip of warm light underneath; Kessler is preparing the third thing. Reveals Ch11.
foreshadowing.payoff_note: D-10 launch announcement (Sol 70 1000 briefing) IS the third thing Kessler was preparing.

foreshadowing.note: Kessler asks about Peter's grandfather's name on resume (Ch7 plant continuing).
foreshadowing.payoff_note: Kessler asks Peter's grandfather's name; Peter answers 'Kamau Mwangi', 'Mombasa'.

foreshadowing.note: Stellar drift millions of years requirement — pseudo-resolved by cover story (CTC entrance/exit anchoring across deep time).
Vasquez 'higher than you'll need' — pseudo-resolved as Casimir cradle headroom.

Reference epochs in catch-side-pass-1 cluster tightly around a single point in deep time, narrower than the cover-story's design tolerance permits. A line, not a smear. Peter has not yet decoded what epoch.
foreshadowing.payoff_note: Sol 66 0220: catalogue match returns 67 million years / Cretaceous. The cluster-not-smear was the Chicxulub epoch.

foreshadowing.note: Peter pulls down the stellar-cartography comparison tool he has not used since Helix and queries the cluster's barycentric center against historical epoch catalogues on his eight-year-old datapad. A small progress bar opens, where the warning flag used to live, and begins to load.
foreshadowing.payoff_note: Sol 65 night: bar at 100%. Sol 66 0220: result line read three times; reference epoch barycentric center matched to Chicxulub catalogue; verified via two reruns (inverted-frame-stripped-metadata and naive).

foreshadowing.note: The coral fragment is grandmother Miriam's village relic; Earth's last reef Andaman Fragment is dead; Kessler's grief is real. Anchors the moral-weight half of the climax decision.
foreshadowing.recontextualization_note: Coral fragment recontextualized: it is the engine of Kessler motivation, not a Miriam memento. Grief is real; the object signifies grief-into-action. Peter recognition Sol 67 late afternoon Nav Lab. Anchors Vol 3 moral-weight axis.
foreshadowing.carry_note_ch11: Coral fragment doubled: Kessler brings it to briefing (Sol 70 1000) and it is back on its glass stand in his office (Sol 70 late afternoon). Hand-near-coral: lay near it the way a man's hand lay near a child he was not going to wake. The care is for the next ten days.

foreshadowing.note: Peter privately recognizes Kessler as a possible future self — both brilliant men discarded by institutions; the only difference, he understands, is that Kessler chose action and Peter spent six months in a Mars apartment closing job applications. Vol3+ payoff; sets up the climax decision frame.
foreshadowing.carry_note_ch10: Mirror-recognition (Ch9) becomes monstrous in retrospect; Peter remembers seeing Kessler as a possible future self for two short sentences and does not finish the thought. Vol 3 climax-decision frame.
foreshadowing.carry_note_ch11: Light touch line 77: "a man in his last week of being unopposed".

foreshadowing.note: Kessler's wife left in 2527 because there was no longer a place to live and she was not, fairly, an evolutionary biologist. Single mention. Vol3 light payoff potential.
foreshadowing.carry_note_ch11: Untouched in Ch11; defer to Ch13.

foreshadowing.note: Peter has knowledge, access, and the one skill that could stop the plan. He does not have certainty. Not yet. Central Vol 3 question: what will Peter do?
foreshadowing.advancement_note: Decision formed: Peter has the certainty he lacked at Ch10 close. Vol 3 axis converted from will-he? to how-in-five-days?

foreshadowing.note: Screen corner where the warning flag lived (Ch7-8) and the progress bar lived (Ch9-10) is now blank. Motif retired to blank; Vol 3 may revisit if Peter reopens the comparison tool or re-instruments the integrator.
foreshadowing.carry_note_ch11: Bookend: blank at line 7 (0700 open of day) and blank at line 97 (2210 close of day). Motif retained.

foreshadowing.note: Sol 68 bunk: stars are exactly where they should be -- for now. Sets up Vol 3 Ch15 nothing-changed monitoring beat.
foreshadowing.carry_note_ch11: Sol 68 wedge advanced 2 days to Sol 70: Vega, Alpha Centauri pair, Milky Way, Pleiades just out of frame to the left where they will be for the next ten days. Vol 3 Ch15 setup intact.

foreshadowing.note: Peter passed Vasquez Sol 67 integration window without his face changing. Sets up Ch12 code-review trust: Vasquez does not yet suspect.
foreshadowing.carry_note_ch11: Vasquez schedules Sol 75 1400 walkthrough without suspicion; Sol 68 one-line congratulation on clean residual run referenced. Trust foundation for Ch12 code review.

foreshadowing.note: Sol 75 D-5 Vasquez nav-stack walkthrough deadline. Five days from Ch11 close to convert mental insertion points into reviewable code.

Three integrator reference-frame priority sites identified mentally Sol 70 late afternoon Nav Lab. Not written down (he had practice in not writing things down). Read-only mental cataloging in Ch11; Ch12 will write code.
Peter's private resolve crystallized in Kessler's office under warm light across hand near coral. Vol 3 axis converted from will-he? to how-in-five-days?

hook.content: Marcus files regulatory inquiry but it will take weeks; Freeman is already preparing a ship to the outer solar system. The accelerator will be long gone.

ending.location: Prometheus Facility / transport pod leaving Olympus City

hook.content: Three stolen technologies loaded onto one shuttle for the Kuiper Belt. A job listing appears for a planetary navigation programmer — the final piece has no idea what he is walking into.

ending.location: Unmarked launch pad, outskirts of Olympus City / Mars (implied Peter location)

hook.content: Peter accepts the mysterious recruitment, unknowingly becoming the final piece of a plan involving three stolen technologies. Reader knows it is a trap.

Peter opens his project specs and finds navigation parameters far beyond asteroid mining — dozens of stellar reference epochs, terawatt-range power conduits, precision tolerances for extreme durations, gravitational models for unknown bodies, autonomous operation windows beyond any commercial scope. The pattern is wrong.

ending.location: Nav Lab, Kuiper Belt Asteroid Base

hook.content: Peter writes three questions on the paper notepad: Why does the nav require stellar positions millions of years deep? Whose mining trajectory has ever needed that? Ask Arthur. Tomorrow morning. First thing. Turns the page facedown; the yellow warning flag flickers and resolves. Decision-point variant: tomorrows answer will reframe everything.

ending.location: Nav Lab, Kuiper Belt Asteroid Base

hook.content: Peter starts catch-side-pass-1 under the cover-story frame; the model holds cleanly; the soft yellow warning flag in the corner of his left display clears, and he does not notice it go. End-of-quarter deliverables (launch trajectory phase, then interceptor approach geometry) and a deferred 'third thing' pull him forward — under dramatic irony, since the orbital interceptors do not exist.

pattern.opening_note: postponement compression Sol 45-49, then Sol 50 commit

ending.location: Nav Lab, Kuiper Belt Asteroid Base

hook.content: Peter, on a habit of attention he could not have explained at the time, asks the integrator a different question — and finds the reference epochs are not distributed across the cover-storys million-year envelope but clustered tightly around a single point in deep time. A line, not a smear. He pulls down the stellar-cartography comparison tool he has not run since Helix and queries the historical epoch catalogues on his eight-year-old datapad. A small progress bar opens, quietly, where the warning flag used to live, and begins to load.

pattern.opening_note: multi-day belonging compression Sol 51-57 + Sol 58 1830 corridor catch

ending.location: Nav Lab, Kuiper Belt Asteroid Base

hook.content: Sol 68 0140 cache deletion + bunk paralysis under observation port stars: Peter has the knowledge, the access, and the one skill that could stop the plan. He does not have the certainty. Not yet. The unwritten conditional left as the shape of a thing in the next room.

pattern.opening_note: Loading bar at four percent, same chair as Ch9 close; Sol 58→65 compression; Sol 66 0220 reveal

ending.location: Bunk, residential level, Kuiper Belt Asteroid Base (observation port stars in frame)

hook.content: Five days, a clean build, three insertion points held only in memory, and the rest of his life inside one of two skies. He had five days to write a plan. He went to his bunk.

ending.location: Bunk, residential level, Kuiper Belt Asteroid Base

history.note: Accelerator acquisition thread advanced - Freeman secures Kepler-7
Sun 2.0 theft completes third acquisition; all three pieces converge on single shuttle; Peter recruitment hook drops — constellation of plot threads crystallizes
Peter recruited as final piece — quest thread advances (navigation programmer acquired); fire thread ignites (Peter character introduction, emotional depth, AI displacement theme)
Peter arrives at asteroid base (quest advances); discovers three stolen technologies in situ and anomalous project specs (constellation thread -- all pieces now visible to POV character)

Kessler introduction is the chapter’s emotional core (fire thread ignites: warmth, recognition, mentor seduction); meanwhile mounting evidence — coral fragment, deep-time vocabulary, accelerator overspecification, nanomachine swarm intimacy, Kessler’s political end-run around Samir — sharpens the constellation of red flags. Peter’s notepad of three questions and quiet resolve to ask Arthur tomorrow is the cliff into Ch8.

Quest: Peter advances on the actual project deliverables (launch trajectory phase + interceptor approach geometry; catch-side-pass-1 branch opened). Constellation: cover-story canon binds Kerr black hole / Casimir cradle / Sun 2.0 / Kepler-7 / von Neumann probe / orbital interceptors / Vasquez / nano programmer bay into one architecture, all visible to POV — orbital interceptors plant sits under dramatic irony for Ch10 reversal.

Fire: Kessler personal reveal (Earth six times / Miriam / Andaman / defunding / wife / build-something-instead) + Peter mirror-recognition + Peter-Oren-Vasquez crew warmth — single largest emotional-core beat of Vol2. Quest: catch-side-pass-1 nearly complete; Sol 58 routine consistency check exposes epoch cluster as a line not a smear; Peter activates clandestine investigation by loading Helix comparison tool against historical epoch catalogues.

Quest: catch-side-pass-1 refactor delivered Sol 65; Sol 66 0220 query result returns 67M years/Cretaceous; trajectory frame inversion identifies Chicxulub impactor as deflection target; 0.01-degree adjustment recontextualized as the entire point of the code; Sol 68 0140 cache deletion preserves optionality. Constellation: cover story collapses into actual plan -- Kerr/CTC/Casimir-cradle/Sun2.0/Kepler-7/von Neumann probe/0.01-degree all bind into ablation-of-humanity architecture; orbital interceptors confirmed nonexistent; coral fragment recontextualized engine-not-memento; Peter-Kessler mirror becomes monstrous in retrospect. Vol 2 close.

Fire: Oren blue-cup small-warmth beat; Kessler warm office conversation under dramatic irony; Peter small laugh at dinner; the small-human surplus that survives the deadline. Quest: Sol 70 D-10 launch announcement; Vasquez Sol 75 D-5 walkthrough scheduled; Sol 70 late afternoon Nav Lab read-through identifies three reference-frame priority insertion points (mental only); private decision crystallizes. Vol 3 opener; central axis converted from will-he? to how-in-five-days? Constellation strand has now rested 2 chapters (Ch10 last); flag for Ch13-14 reactivation.

open_questions.q: Can he insert anything into the code without Vasquez detecting it?
How much time does he have? (Ch11 reveals D-10)
open_questions.resolution: D-10 announced Sol 70 briefing. Sol 80 launch.
D-10 launch announcement IS the third thing.

open_questions.q: Does Kessler grief make him a monster?
How will Peter insert the bug without Vasquez detecting during Sol 75 walkthrough?
Can the bug survive review + 1 million years of autonomous operation?
